title: Biased Auctioneers
abstract: We construct a neural network algorithm that generates price predictions for art at auction, relying on both visual and nonvisual object characteristics. We find that higher automated valuations relative to auction house presale estimates are associated with substantially higher price-to-estimate ratios and lower buy-in rates, pointing to estimates' informational inefficiency. The relative contribution of machine learning is higher for artists with less dispersed and lower average prices. Furthermore, we show that auctioneers' prediction errors are persistent both at the artist and at the auction house level, and hence directly predictable themselves using information on past errors.

article.introduction:
Auction houses do not simplymatch consignors to bidders. They also act as brokers of information. In particular, they publicly communicate market value estimates of the lots for sale. While auction theory suggests that “honesty is the best policy” (Milgrom and Weber (1982)), at least for a monopolistic auctioneer, there are good reasons to think that presale estimates may not be unbiased. In particular, art and other collectibles' illiquidity and heterogeneity make the task of valuation far from obvious (Chambers, Dimson, and Spaenjers (2020)), and both behavioral frictions and strategic-competitive considerations can impact auction houses' proclaimed valuations.
  To shed light on whether any given behavioral or strategic bias systematically skews estimates, one can correlate auctioneers' prediction errors with a proxy for the determinant of the hypothesized bias.1Consider the following simple example. Prior work suggests that intermediaries in real asset markets are slow to adjust their appraisals, especially downwards. Such behavior can be traced back to cognitive biases, but may also reflect strategic incentives to avoid signaling market downturns to collector-investors (Brown and Matysiak (2000), Velthuis (2007), Dimson and Spaenjers (2011)). We can thus hypothesize that recent price trends—especially adverse ones—will not always be reflected in auction house estimates. Using art sales data presented later in this paper, we test this hypothesis by first computing artist-level average annualized returns on resales over the period 2008 to 2014 (for items for which we can identify an initial transaction since 2003). We then use this measure to sort all auctioned lots by the same sample of artists in 2015. Figure1plots the distributions of logged price-to-estimate ratios in 2015 for the quartiles of lots with the worst and best artist-level investment performance in recent years. As can be clearly seen, auctioneers' ex ante value assessments are more likely to overshoot (undershoot) ex post transaction prices for artists with low (high) recent returns.
  Exercises such as these can help identify specific drivers of auctioneers' prediction errors. However, the pattern shown in Figure1may coexist with many other sources of systematic variation in the data, some of which may be hard to precisely and separately define and measure. Furthermore, estimates may have an idiosyncratic error component as well. Any single cut of the data thus tells us little about the overall informational efficiency of presale estimates. One way to gauge whether presale estimates can be improved upon, as economically meaningful predictors of cross-sectional variation in transaction prices, is to come up with reasonable counterfactual estimates that auctioneerscould have pickedgiven their information set. Yet, constructing such benchmark valuations is not straightforward when relying on standard statistical tools such as linear regression models, particularly when these models are estimated on relatively small data sets. This may explain the conflicting conclusions in prior work on auction house biases (see Ashenfelter and Graddy (2006) for a review of the literature).
  Our paper makes progress on this front by creating a novel statistical algorithm that generates automated valuations of artworks based on a large database of past auction outcomes. To do so, we build on recent advances in machine learning and computer vision.2A conceptual rather than methodological novelty of our paper is that we use our technology not only to generate predictions ofpricesthat can be compared to auction house estimates, but also to directly generate predictions ofauctioneers' valuation errors. Like our benchmark analysis, this statistical approach to studying estimates' informational efficiency does not require any prior hypothesis or knowledge on what biases auction houses are subject to. Of course, auction house prediction errors will be predictable out-of-sample only to the extent that such biases and the resulting errors are sufficiently persistent. To our knowledge, our paper is the first to examine the persistence and predictability of auctioneers' under- and overvaluation patterns.
  Our research starts from data on 1.2 million painting auctions from a proprietary database of art sales. The data capture the near-totality of global art auction activity over our sample period from 2008 to 2015. For each lot, the database contains detailed information on the artist, the artwork, and the auction. The data also include an image of each item. Nearly every item in our database has low and high presale estimates that were issued by the auction house; we work with the mean value in our analysis. If the item sells at auction, we observe its hammer price; otherwise, we know that it has been “bought in.” The distribution of art prices has a very long right tail: While the median hammer price in our data set is $3,271, the average equals $61,225. Our initial data set includes hundreds of auction houses, but the top three (Christie's, Sotheby's, and Bonhams) account for 22% of all observations and 70% of aggregate dollar volume.
  Using data for the period 2008 to 2014, we generate price predictions for artworks auctioned in 2015 using machine learning. Specifically, we train a neural network, which can be seen as a very large parametric model in which the parameters are “learned” from observations in an iterative and stochastic manner. Because we want to use the image of each item, we estimate a “convolutional” neural network, a type of neural network that is often used for image-recognition tasks. In addition to the image, our benchmark prediction relies on independent variables derived from textual and numerical data in the database (e.g., artist, artwork materials, auction house). When constructing and estimating the model, we build in a number of features that help avoid overfitting, particularly with respect to the artist dummies.
  To be useful as counterfactual presale valuations, our machine learning price predictions need to be sufficiently accurate. We verify the accuracy in an out-of-sample test set of auctions that took place in 2015, after filtering out artists and auction houses that are economically of minor importance. We find that our automated valuations explain nearly 75% of the transaction price variation in the test sample. We then let the neural network make new predictions after dropping different (sets of) variables and examine the change inR2. We find that artist-related information is much more relevant for price predictions than artwork properties. The incremental explanatory power of images is relatively limited, at least after conditioning on artist and artwork characteristics.
  As a benchmark for our novel machine learning valuations, we also use a standard linear hedonic model to generate price predictions for all test lots. When relating price outcomes to the hedonic valuations, we obtain anR2of 67.7%. This compares to 74.2% for the predictions based on our neural network. Because of the lack of interaction effects in a standard hedonic model, machine learning valuations are much more likely to be accurate than hedonic valuations for works by high-volume and high-dispersion artists—who also tend to be more expensive on average.
  Not surprisingly, even our most sophisticated automated valuations are worse “predictors” of transaction prices than the auctioneers' presale estimates (R2above 90%). Auction house experts have access to more qualitative information about the artwork (e.g., condition, provenance) and about the artist's place in art history than our algorithms. Also, if potential buyers anchor their valuations on publicly available auction house estimates, then the estimates may be endogenously correlated with price outcomes. Thus, we cannot conclude from our ex post comparison of predictive power that humans beat machines in the task of art price prediction. (The goal of this paper is not to set up such a “horse race.”)
  We next study whether the relative level of our novel machine-generated price predictions (compared to auction house estimates) predicts relative price outcomes. We show that after conditioning on presale estimates, our machine learning valuations have economically and statistically significant explanatory power for price-to-estimate ratios. We also expect an effect on buy-in rates, as consignors typically set their reserve slightly below the low estimate provided by the auction house, implying a strong correlation between the two. Indeed, when our automated valuation is high relative to the auction house estimate, the buy-in probability is only around 25%, while this probability exceeds 45% when the prediction generated by the machine learning algorithm is low relative to the auctioneer's valuation.
  We dig deeper into the determinants of the relative usefulness of machine-based valuations, which is determined jointly by the accuracy of our automated predictions and the accuracy of the auction house estimates. We find that our machine learning predictions—but not human experts—become predictably more accurate for objects by artists with a narrow range of price levels and for artworks that, based on their characteristics, can be expected to be easier to value in an automated fashion. Our neural network price predictions are also more likely to be more accurate than presale estimates for less expensive artists, artists historically associated with high prediction errors by auctioneers, high-volume artists, and more recent artworks.3
  If auctioneers are affected by systematic biases, then it might be possible to predict ex ante situations in which auctioneers' valuations are likely to be too optimistic or too pessimistic. We show that prediction errors are indeed persistent at both the artist and the auction house level. We then use our statistical framework to directly predict auctioneers' prediction errors. More precisely, we generate ex ante predictions of the price-to-estimate ratio for each lot, using exactly the same inputs as before (including the image), and of the presale estimate. We find a substantial correlation between predicted and actual deviations of transaction prices from estimates. Moreover, the buy-in rate decreases sharply in thepredictedprice-to-estimate ratio. This predictability exists even if the network does not have access to the auctioneer's estimate. Both the identity of the artist and the identity of the auctioneer are important factors driving this predictability, suggesting that behavioral biases and strategic considerations may each play a role.
  Finally, we note that nonfundamental variation in auction house presale estimates has real economic effects as it drives heterogeneity in art market participants' investment outcomes. Because consignors' reserve prices are strongly correlated with auction house estimates, buy-in rates are higher if auctioneers are more optimistic. But bidders may also anchor on auction house estimates. We can therefore expect artworks associated with more aggressive estimates—relative to our automated valuations—to have lower future returns.4We find suggestive evidence in support of this hypothesis using data on artwork resales over the period 2016 to 2018 for which the purchase is in our 2015 test set.
  While the empirical setting studied in this paper is the art auction market—for which large amounts of historical data on both prices and human experts' valuations are available—investigating the predictability of both prices and biases in intermediaries' information provision clearly carries relevance for other real asset markets as well. Consider, in particular, real estate. While automated valuation models are increasingly common in the housing market, as illustrated by the rise of “iBuyers” (Buchak et al. (2022)), not much is known about their predictive power for transaction values or about the heterogeneity therein. In addition, behavioral frictions and incentive issues can affect housing appraisals (Salzman and Zwinkels (2017)), and real estate market participants may anchor their valuations on those of human experts.
  The remainder of this paper is organized as follows. SectionIdiscusses the empirical setting. SectionIIpresents the data. SectionIIIintroduces our machine learning algorithm, while SectionIVassesses its predictive power. SectionVpresents evidence that auction house estimates are informationally inefficient, and SectionVIshows that auctioneers' prediction errors are themselves predictable. SectionVIIdiscusses key takeaways and implications of our findings.
article.I. Art Auctions and Presale Estimates:
Art auctions are typically organized as “English” (i.e., ascending-bid, open-outcry) auctions. Each consignor sets a reserve price, which is the lowest price she is willing to accept, in agreement with the auction house.
  If the highest bid at the auction meets or exceeds the reserve price, the object will be sold at this price—the hammer price.5If the highest bid remains below the reserve price, the item is said to be bought in; it does not sell and instead returns to the consignor.
  Prior to most auctions, auction house experts publicly share low and high estimates for each lot. Artworks' market values are difficult to determine because every object is unique and trades only infrequently. Nonetheless, many thousands of objects are sold publicly at auction every year. Art auction sales databases thus contain a lot of information about collectors' willingness to pay, and auctioneers consider recent auction prices for similar works.
  Auction house estimates are said to represent auction house experts' opinion “about the range in which the lot might sell at auction” (both quotes in this paragraph are taken from Sotheby's website). Presale estimates indeed appear to be relatively accurate on average, at least when taking into account buy-ins (McAndrew, Smith, and Thompson (2012)). At the same time, however, auction houses typically specify that their estimates serve only as “an approximate guide to current market value and should not be interpreted as a representation or prediction of actual selling prices.” The latter statement suggests that auction houses may sometimes strategically choose to be relatively aggressive or conservative in their estimates. On the one hand, higher estimates may help attract consignors away from competitors (Gammon (2019)) or increase bids by “credulous” investors on certain lots (Mei and Moses (2005)). On the other hand, lower estimates may steer consignors to lower reserves (thereby increasing sale rates) or attract bidders to the auction. Thus, presale estimates may not necessarily represent auctioneers' honest assessment of what they think the hammer price will be.
  In addition to (conscious) strategic biases, (unconscious) behavioral biases in expectations formation may affect auctioneers' choice of presale estimates. Prior research on illiquid real asset markets shows that investors and intermediaries may suffer from extrapolation bias (Glaeser and Nathanson (2017)), reference dependence (Genesove and Mayer (2001), Andersen et al. (2022)), anchoring (Beggs and Graddy (2009)), and confirmation bias (Eriksen et al. (2020)), among other biases.
  Different strategic and behavioral biases can of course aggregate and interact in complex ways. Moreover, auctioneers can make idiosyncratic errors.6If the cumulative effect of these factors on auctioneers' estimates is sufficiently large, then it may be possible to improve on these estimates as predictors of (cross-sectional variation in) transaction prices. Moreover, if the auctioneers' biases and the resulting prediction errors are persistent, then it should be possible to pick up predictability in the distribution of deviations of ex post transaction prices from ex ante auction house estimates. We study both of these hypotheses in our empirical analysis.
article.II. Data:
The analysis in this paper relies on proprietary data that come from the Blouin Art Sales Index, which tracks auction sales at hundreds of auction houses worldwide, including at Christie's and Sotheby's. This database is used previously by Korteweg, Kräussl, and Verwijmeren (2016). Since the end of 2007, the data also include information on buy-ins. Here, we use data on paintings offered at auctions over the period 2008 to 2015.7In total, our data set contains information on 1.2 million lots at hundreds of auction houses of works by approximately 130,000 individual artists.
  For each lot, the database contains information on the artist (artist name, nationality, birth and death years, style), the artwork (year of creation, size, materials, title, markings such as signature or date), and the auction (auction house, auction location, auction date, presale low and high estimates, buy-in indicator, hammer price if sold). All price data are converted to U.S. dollars using the spot rate at the time of the sale. Uniquely, we also have access to a high-quality image of each painting.
  About two-thirds of the auction lots in our sample were sold, while the remaining one-third were bought in because the highest bid was below the consignor's reserve price. TableIsummarizes hammer prices for the overall data set as well as for the top three most frequent artists, auction houses, and auction locations in our data set. The average hammer price is $61,225 while the median is $3,271, indicating a long right tail of very expensive paintings. The two top-selling artists over our sample period, Pablo Picasso and Andy Warhol, both have a mean hammer price exceeding one million dollars. Prices are also clearly above average at Christie's and Sotheby's, which are the two auction houses with the highest number of sales, followed by Bonhams. While Paris is the most frequently observed auction location, prices are substantially lower there than in New York and London. Taken together, the top three auction houses and auction locations account for 22% and 28% of observations, and 70% and 73% of aggregate dollar volume, respectively.
  TableIalso presents some statistics on price-to-estimate ratios, which we compute by dividing the hammer price by the mean—or “mid”—presale estimate (and subsequently winsorizing at 0.10 and 10). We find that the median (mean) price-to-estimate ratio associated with successful sales is slightly below (above) one. The interquartile range is substantial, ranging from prices 29% below the mid estimate to 26% above. (Moreover, for all buy-ins, the highest bid is substantially below the mid estimate, as reserve prices cannot exceed the low estimate.8)
  Figure2plots time trends for key statistics. Panel A shows the number of observations and sale rate (i.e., one minus the percentage of buy-ins) for each year in our sample period. Panel B shows the yearly mean and median hammer price and price-to-estimate ratio. In the next sections, we use data for the final year in our sample period to test the predictive power of different valuations. It is thus reassuring that Figure2shows no dramatic changes in sample composition for 2015.
article.III. Valuing Art Using Machine Learning:
In this section, we explain how we generate alternative valuations that auctioneerscould have setgiven the information that they had access to at the time they decided on their presale estimates. We use artworks auctioned over the period 2008 to 2014 to develop an algorithm that predicts the price of any artwork based on its characteristics. In SectionIV, we test the performance of our algorithm using auction data from 2015. Our out-of-sample test follows our training period to avoid using information from after a sale to predict its outcome.
  The machine learning technique we employ is a neural network, which can be thought of as a very large parametric model. Neural networks consist of different interconnected “layers” of nodes (or “neurons”). The first layer—the “input layer”—represents the variables from which predictions are made, while the final layer—the “output layer”—contains predictions based on the inputs. Between the input and output layers are layers that apply linear mappings and nonlinear “activation functions” to the nodes in the previous layer, which intuitively can be viewed as extracting the information that is most helpful for making predictions. Neural networks' parameters, also called weights, are typically “learned” from observations in an iterative and stochastic manner.
  Given that our data set contains not only textual information on artworks' characteristics but also their images, we use a type of neural network that is popular in image-recognition tasks, namely, a convolutional neural network (CNN). Such networks can learn complex functions of images' pixel values, while taking advantage of the spatial structure of an image in which nearby pixels are correlated. CNNs have been shown to be able to “predict” an artwork's genre, creator, and semantic content, as well as human aesthetic judgments (Karayev et al. (2014), Tan et al. (2016), Strezoski and Worring (2017)). Thus, in theory, our algorithm can pick up any relation between artwork subject and composition (shape, color, etc.) on the one hand and prices on the other hand.
  The following subsections detail the input variables that enter the algorithm, the architecture of the neural network, and our estimation of the network.
  Appendix TableA.Iprovides summary statistics for the different input variables described above.
  The architecture of our neural network is depicted in Figure3. In the input layer, each nonvisual input variable is represented by a vector of dummies, which in machine learning is typically referred to as “one-hot encoding.” We therefore have separate indicator variables for each artist, each artist nationality, and so forth.9For each input variable category (see items i to xiii above), we then project this initial representation onto a 10-dimensional vector.10This projection is conducted using a combination of a linear operation (a fully connected layer whose parameters are learned during training) and a nonlinear activation function called a rectified linear unit (ReLU).11There are several reasons for introducing this 10-dimensional bottleneck. First, it reduces the number of network parameters. Second, it avoids overfitting related to the high dimensionality of some of the initial dummy variable representations (e.g., artist, auction house). The 10-dimensional projection can be seen as encouraging the network to consider many different artists or auction houses in a similar way. Third, associating 10 dimensions to each type of input variable, including low-dimensional ones (e.g., the variable category measuring markings, which only has three dummies), ensures that our algorithm does not attach more importance to variables that are initially associated with more dimensions.
  We also represent the images with a 10-dimensional vector that is computed using a so-called ResNet. ResNets are one of the most common CNN architectures for images (He et al. (2016)). Here, we use a network type known to have good performance for image classification while being small enough to be trained in a reasonable amount of time.
  The vector that appends the intermediate representations of the nonvisual input variables and the image is then used as an input in a “multi-layer perceptron” (MLP) with one hidden layer (with 100 nodes). This MLP is the function that makes the price predictions by applying linear and nonlinear operations to the 10-dimensional intermediate representations of the input variables.
  The total number of parameters in our network architecture is in the order of one million. Most correspond to the conversion of the artist dummies into a 10-dimensional representation. Approximately 15,000 correspond to the MLP that generates price predictions starting from the intermediate representations. (Note that the different parts of the network are trained—and the weights are thus optimized—simultaneously.) Intuitively, therefore, our network is not prone to much overfitting, because—helped by the initial projections of all input variables into 10-dimensional representations—the number of parameters in the prediction function is much smaller than the number of observations in the training set.
  The network is estimated using the nearly one million lots over the 2008 to 2014 period. A randomly sampled 1% of these observations is used as validation data. The performance of the network on this small subsample is used during the development phase to decide on meta-parameters related to both the network architecture (e.g., the dimensionality of the intermediate variable representations, the number of hidden layers and nodes in the MLP) and the optimization.
  We train the algorithms on hammer prices, but we also include buy-ins at an imputed price equal to 75% of the auction house's low estimate motivated by our knowledge of average reserve-to-estimate ratios (e.g., McAndrew, Smith, and Thompson (2012)). All prices are log-transformed. Moreover, given we want to focus on economically meaningful variation in art prices—and we perform our tests on data for auction houses and artists with at least a minimal level of recognition—we winsorize all prices at $1,000 prior to training.12
  To regularize our training, we set each variable to zero with a probability of 0.2. This process of randomly zeroing out a subset of the input variables during training is similar in spirit to dropout procedures more typically applied to intermediary nodes in the network to avoid overfitting (Srivastava et al. (2014)). Our approach trains the network so that it can make predictions even if some information about the artist or artwork is missing. It also enables us to study the relative impact of the different variables on predictions, and to see how removing certain information changes the predictive power of the network.
  One of the caveats of deep learning networks such as ours is that it can be difficult to understand what exactly the intermediate representations are learning. In our relatively parsimonious architecture, this is especially true for the 10-dimensional vector of image features that the network generates from the original pixel values. To provide insight into this issue, Figure4displays a set of artworks (first column) and their “nearest neighbors” (subsequent columns) based on the image features. More precisely, for 10 images (by 10 different high-volume artists), we identify the nine other images in the training data with the highest (cosine) similarities. The figure shows that the way in which the network considers different artworks as “similar” is relatively complex, including elements of both semantic content and style.13
article.IV. Assessing Machine Learning Valuation:
In this section, we verify how well the price predictions generated by our neural network line up with actual transaction prices out-of-sample. We also compare the predictive power of our baseline model to a number of alternative estimates and algorithms. We do so using data for (a subset of all) artworks auctioned in 2015, which the network did not “see” while learning. We impose two data filters. First, we drop a small fraction (0.5%) of sales in which the hammer price is below 10% of the low estimate or above 10 times the high estimate. Some of these outliers may be cases where either the price or the estimate is incorrectly recorded in the database, or some (to us) unobservable event happened between estimation and auction of the artwork (e.g., a re-attribution).14Second, to ensure that our analysis focuses on economically meaningful objects and trading places, we focus on artworks by artists and auction houses that are associated with an average mid estimate over the training period of at least $5,000 and $10,000, respectively. This filter reduces the number of observations in our test set by roughly two-thirds, but in terms of aggregate dollar sales the filtered-out lots represent less than 5% of the initial sample. The final data set for the tests in this section still contains nearly 60,000 auction sales at 81 different auction houses in 78 locations on five continents, for works by more than 12,000 different artists.
  Denote byPthe (log) realized hammer prices for the objects in our test set. If a work is bought in, we impute a value of 75% of the low estimate. The variableMLdenotes the out-of-sample (log) price predictions generated by the neural network for each year-2015 auction. In Panel A of Figure5, we compare the distributions of these two variables for all observations in our test set. The machine learning predictions exhibit less dispersion in the left tail, which can be explained by the winsorization applied during the training of the neural network.
  Panel B of Figure5shows the distribution of prediction errorsP−ML$P - ML$(winsorized at −4 and +4). We see a rather nicely behaved bell curve with a mean and median just below zero. The interquartile range runs from −0.70 to 0.36, meaning that half of all prediction errors fall in this interval, which corresponds to deviations of prices from our machine learning predictions of−50%$-50\%$to+43%$+43\%$. The mean (median) absolute prediction error|P−ML|$|P-ML|$equals 0.69 (0.55).
  The scatter plot in Panel C shows how auction pricesPline up with our machine learning valuationsML. We also plot the results of a linear regression model and the predicted linear fit. We see that the regression line is almost exactly 45°—the slope coefficient is almost perfectly one, while the intercept is very close to zero. TheR2shows that our automated valuations explain about three-quarters of the (out-of-sample) variation in auction prices.
  To open up the black box of price formation—or at least price predictability—in the art market, in TableII, we show the predictive power of a number of variations of our benchmark model. These alternative models generate new predictions for all objects in the test data set after removing their values on one or more of the input variable categories introduced in SectionIII. We then recompute theR2based on the (updated) machine learning valuations and the (unchanged) price outcomes. Our approach is a valid one to study the predictive power of models with less inputs, because we explicitly train the algorithm to make predictions even in the presence of missing information.
  As a starting point, the entry in the first row and first column of TableIIrepeats theR2for our benchmark model—74.2%. Subsequent rows show how theR2changes if we drop certain sets of nonvisual inputs. We first drop the auction-related information (auction house, auction location, auction month), which picks up the price predictability due to the endogenous matching of artworks to auctions. By omitting the data associated with this selection stage, we provide evidence on how much predictive power our model would have in a setting in which the place and time of an object's sale is unknown. To see how ex ante predictability is affected by asset-specific features, we omit in subsequent rows different sets of artist or artwork-related characteristics. We see that dropping the artist dummies has a substantial effect on the predictive power of our model, although theR2is reduced only by about one-third as long as the network has information on artist nationality and period. If all information related to the artist and the creation period is removed, theR2drops to only 18.6%. We further find that artwork size and materials matter much more than (our proxy for) artwork title and the presence of a signature or other markings. Removing information on all “physical” artwork characteristics (size, materials, title, markings) reduces predictive power less than simply removing the artist identifiers, at least in this set of variations where the neural network can still rely on the artwork images.
  We next repeat all models without images, leading to predictions that we denote byMLtxt$ML_{txt}$. The resultingR2s are shown in the second column of TableII. One can conclude from the first row that the incremental explanatory power of images is relatively limited—theR2is only 2.4 percentage points lower without images. So either visual characteristics are not important in driving prices (after controlling for artist identity, size, materials, and so on), or economic valueisassociated with certain image characteristics but machine learning is ineffective in identifying such relations. At least two arguments can be made in favor of the first interpretation. First, as we illustrate in Figure4, the algorithm appears to be able to identify visual similarities between different artworks. Second, the last row of TableIIshows that the relative difference in predictive power between models with versus without images becomes much larger after we remove nonvisual artwork descriptors. This result suggests that the network is able to pick up meaningful relations between artwork characteristics and prices based on the images.
  Figure6shows the distribution of hedonic valuations, the distribution of prediction errors, and the relation between hedonic valuations and transaction prices, mirroring the panels of Figure5based on machine learning valuations. In Panel A, we find that the distribution of hedonic valuations is more concentrated than that of prices—and also than that of machine learning valuations. In Panel C, we see that theR2is substantially lower than before, at 67.7% compared to 74.2%. The predicted linear fit is also further away from a 45° benchmark.
  A major conceptual difference between a machine learning approach and a hedonic model is that the latter does not exploit interaction effects between different variables. Thus, after conditioning on important determinants of variation in price levels, the hedonic valuations will show little dispersion. We illustrate this in Figure7, which provides a number of histograms for works by Pablo Picasso in our test set. Panel A provides histograms for hedonic valuationsHR, Panel B for our earlier generated neural network price predictionsML, and Panel C for price outcomesP. Each panel includes two distributions of predictions or prices for oil paintings (based on whether the artwork is relatively large or small), and two distributions for nonoil artworks (based on whether the lot was auctioned at one of the two major auction houses or not).
  Figure7shows that prices are affected by the materials and size of the artwork, and correlate with the identity of the auction house—and that these patterns are reflected in bothHRandML. We also see, however, that the dispersion in prices for Picasso works is little reflected in the hedonic valuations. Each of the four categories of works considered is associated with a narrow distribution ofHR. This is not surprising given the additive and linear structure of a standard hedonic model. For example, all small Picasso oil paintings will have relatively similar hedonic valuations, driven mainly by the (aggregation of the) coefficients on the artist, material, and size variables in the model. Our machine learning valuationsMLexhibit much more variation, even within each subsample. While it is difficult to speculate on what exactly drives this variation, it is necessarily related to (potentially artist-specific) interactions between different artwork characteristics—including visual ones—that the neural network identifies as predictive of prices.
  Given the above, we might expect machine learning to be particularly useful—relative to standard statistical tools—for artists associated with a large and varied oeuvre. We address this conjecture in Figure8. We first classify all lots in our test data set into 10 deciles according to the associated artist's number of auction lots in the training data. We then compute the average absolute prediction errors based on bothMLandHRfor each decile, and also compute the fraction of observations for whichMLis closer toPthanHR. The results are presented in Panel A. Panel B then repeats this exercise but uses deciles based on the artist-level standard deviation of (log) transaction prices in the training data as a proxy for the variation in an artist's output.
  We see that our machine learning valuations are indeed more likely to be more accurate than hedonic valuations for works by high-volume and high-dispersion artists. Such artists also tend to be more expensive on average, meaning that machine learning will be more helpful for the economically more important lots.17Panel A of Figure8additionally illustrates that prediction errors forMLare lower—even in absolute terms—for artists with more transactions historically. Panel B shows that our neural network struggles to accurately price artists with a high standard deviation of prices, even if it does much better than a hedonic model.
article.V. Testing the Informational Efficiency:
In this section, we analyze how helpful our machine learning valuations are in the presence of presale valuations issued by the auction house organizing the sale. We denote byEthe (log) mean presale estimate. Similar to Figures5and6, Figure9presents the distribution ofEand of prediction errorsP−E$P-E$, as well as results of a simple linear regression ofPonE. What is striking in Panel A is the discontinuities in the distribution ofE, which is due to the fact that auction houses use standard estimate intervals (e.g., 1 to 2 million, 1.5 to 2.5 million).
  The distribution of prediction errorsP−E$P-E$(i.e., logged price-to-estimate ratios) in Panel B is peaking around −0.5. This reflects the presence of buy-ins, for which we impute prices at 75% of thelowestimate. For about 10% of our observations, the prediction errorP−E$P-E$exceeds 0.5, which is equivalent to the hammer price exceeding the presale estimate by at least 65%.
  Panel C of Figure9shows that auction house estimates explain substantially more of the variation in hammer prices than our machine learning algorithm. We stress, however, that an ex post comparison of predictive power between auction house estimates and our automated valuations should not be construed as a horse race between “man” and “machine.” On the one hand, as explained above, an estimate cannot be considered the auction house's truthful expectation of what the item will sell for. On the other hand, two factors will artificially drive up the relative performance of human-generated estimates. First, auctioneers take into account artwork-level information (e.g., condition, provenance) and artist-level information (e.g., art-historical reputation) that is not observed by our algorithms, even when this information could in principle be quantified and fed to the machine. Second, auction houses' estimates will be endogenously correlated with prices if bidders anchor on those estimates to form beliefs about future resale revenues. (We explore the implications of this possibility at the end of the paper.)
  As we expected, prices are more highly correlated with presale estimates than with the predictions generated by our neural network. However, our primary goal is to analyze whether,conditional on pre-sale estimates, machine learning can help predict auction outcomes. If auctioneers set informationally efficient estimates, this will not be the case. By contrast, if auction house estimates are affected by behavioral or strategic biases, or simply do not reflect all available information, then the relative level ofMLshould predict deviations of transaction prices from presale estimates. In such cases, we can also expect buy-in probabilities to be affected, as reserve prices are typically tightly linked to pre-sale estimates.
  We start our analysis by running a regression of the prediction errorP−E$P - E$againstE. We do so to check whether deviations of prices from presale estimates are on average higher or lower for more valuable items. The results are presented in column (1) of TableIII. We see that realized price deviations from estimates are on average slightly higher for more expensive paintings, but the relation is economically insignificant. In column (2), we addMLorth$ML_{orth}$, which is our machine learning valuationMLthat is orthogonalized with respect toE. (This orthogonalization affects neither the coefficient on theMLvariable nor theR2, but allows us to focus on the additional role that our machine learning valuations play.) Relatively higher machine learning valuations are associated with economically and statistically significantly higher price-to-estimate ratios.
  To illustrate the economic significance of the results in column (2) of TableIII, we depict them visually. We first sort all observations in the test data set onMLorth$ML_{orth}$and group them into half deciles. We then compute the meanMLorth$ML_{orth}$for each of the 20 groups. The line in Figure10shows the mean prediction errorP−E$P-E$(i.e., log price-to-estimate ratio) as a function of the meanMLorth$ML_{orth}$for each half decile. We see that price deviations from presale estimates are on average much higher for higher relative machine learning valuations. For the highest values ofMLorth$ML_{orth}$, the average price—including imputed prices for buy-ins—exceeds the mid estimate. By contrast, for the lowest values ofMLorth$ML_{orth}$, we see thatEexceedsPby more than 0.2 on average.
  So far, we have considered the relation between our predictions and prices. Yet, our finding that we can improve on the presale estimate to predict price outcomes suggests that there might also be some predictability in whether a lot will be bought in. More specifically, if the estimate is set relatively high for a certain work, then the reserve price—decided jointly by auctioneer and consignor, but never above the auctioneer's low estimate—is also likely to be relatively high. We can thus expect to see more buy-ins if our automated valuations are low compared to the presale estimates. We test this hypothesis in columns (3) to (6) of TableIII, which repeat the results for OLS and probit regressions estimated over all lots in the test data set, where the dependent variable is a dummy that equals one if the item was bought in. The negative coefficient onEin each model indicates that in general buy-ins are somewhat less frequent for more expensive art. More importantly, in columns (4) and (6) we see that machine learning artwork valuations help predict buy-ins, in line with our expectation.
  To evaluate the economic significance of our results, in Figure10we plot the realized out-of-sample buy-in frequency as a function of ourMLorth$ML_{orth}$valuations grouped by half-decile as before. The graph shows that the buy-in probability is more than 45% whenMLis low relative to the auction house estimate, while this frequency decreases to around 25% whenMLis relatively high. Thus, the discrepancy between our machine learning valuations and auctioneers' value assessments has substantial predictive power for the probability of selling.
  The analysis above shows that a comparison to machine learning valuations can help assess whether auctioneers' estimates are likely to under- or overshoot bidders' willingness to pay. However, it is likely that there exists heterogeneity in the added value of machine learning. In this subsection, we examine under which conditions we can expect our automated valuations to be more helpful.
  Therelativecontribution of automated valuations depends on both the accuracy of our neural network and the prediction errors of auctioneers. Thus, a first way to approach the issue at hand is to focus on heterogeneity in how well our automated valuations can be expected to predict prices. The results in Panel B of Figure8suggest that ex ante artist-level price dispersion may be a good proxy for the complexity of the task faced by (standard and more sophisticated) machine-based valuation methods. In Panel A of Figure11, we show for each decile of lots in the test set, sorted by artist-level price dispersion in the training set, the mean absolute prediction error of our neural network (i.e.,|P−ML|$|P - ML|$). Furthermore, we show the average absolute prediction error of auctioneers (i.e.,|P−E|$|P - E|$) for each group of lots, and the proportion of lots for whichMLis more accurate thanEas a predictor of the price.18As before, we see that the absolute prediction error of our machine learning algorithm generally rises with artist-level price dispersion. Interestingly, however,|P−E|$|P-E|$does not rise accordingly, meaning that the relative accuracy ofMLdecreases with our proxy for the range of possible prices associated with an artist.
  We construct an alternative proxy for the difficulty of pricing lots in an automated fashion as follows. Using our training data, we regress the (in-sample) absolute prediction errors of our previously presented hedonic model back on all hedonic variables. This allows us to identify which artwork characteristics are associated with less accurate hedonic predictions on average. We then apply the regression coefficients to all observations in the test data set, which gives us an ex ante proxy for each object of how difficult it will be for an automated model to come up with an accurate prediction. We then sort all lots in the test data into deciles based on this measure of the “difficulty for machines.” This approach is in the spirit of Buchak et al. (2022), who aim to identify which kind of properties are hard versus easy to value. The results are shown in Panel B of Figure11, and mirror those in Panel A.19The takeaway is that there exists predictable heterogeneity in the size of the errors made by our neural network, but this variation does not correlate with auctioneers' errors.
  So far, we have attempted to differentiate lots based on whether we can expect them to be easy or difficult to price by an automated algorithm. However, as we explain before, therelativecontribution that we can expect from our machine learning valuations also depends on how accurate we can expect auctioneers' estimates to be—assuming that systematic heterogeneity exists along this dimension. To examine this conjecture, we sort lots in the test set based on the given artist's average|P−E|$|P-E|$in the training data. In effect, we rank artists by the accuracy of auctioneers during the years preceding 2015. The idea is that auction houses may find disentangling the different determinants of cross-sectional and temporal variation in prices persistently more challenging for certain artists than for others. The results are depicted in Panel C of Figure11. We see that the mean absolute prediction error of the presale estimates|P−E|$|P-E|$increases in our newly built proxy for the expected noise in human valuations. However, the machine learning algorithm is also associated with somewhat larger prediction errors when auctioneers' errors go up, and therefore the probability thatMLis more accurate thanEas a predictor ofPincreases only weakly with artists' mean|P−E|$|P-E|$in the training data. One interpretation of this result is that artists associated with higher absolute prediction errors of auctioneers are genuinely more difficult to value on average.
  Finally, in Panel D of Figure11, we sort lots in the test set by artist-level average prices, again measured in the training data. More information—outside of the auction data considered here—is available to auctioneers for more expensive artists. Human prediction errors are indeed slightly smaller for more expensive artists. By contrast,MLbecomes less accurate as the artist's average price goes up, which can be due to more valuable artists' wider dispersion in prices in the auction market (and potentially also the fact that hard-to-quantify factors such as provenance and exhibition history may be more important for more established artists).
  As a more formal test, we run a probit regression in which the dependent variable is a dummy that equals one ifMLis closer toPthanE. In column (1) of TableIV, we include as independent variables the four variables used to sort lots in Figure11. The results are consistent with our earlier results and in the expected directions:MLis likely to be more accurate thanEfor artists with less dispersed and lower average prices, artworks that can be predicted ex ante to be easier to value using a statistical function of artwork characteristics, and artists historically associated with higher absolute prediction errors by auctioneers.
  In column (2) of TableIV, we add two additional variables. First, we add a variable measuring the number of lots in the training data for each artist. Ceteris paribus, we expect that automated valuations are more accurate for more liquid artists. This is indeed what we find. Second, we add a variable measuring the number of years since creation of the artwork. We see that machine learning predictions tend be relatively more accurate for more recent works.
  Finally, in columns (3) and (4) of TableIV, we repeat the models shown in columns (1) and (2) but add auction house fixed effects. The magnitudes and statistical significance of the coefficients do not change much. So the determinants of relative machine learning accuracy that we identify appear to be largely orthogonal to auction house identities. TheR2s go up, however, which suggests a role for auctioneer effects in the magnitude of prediction errors, a question that we turn to next.
article.VI. Predicting Auctioneers' Prediction E:
In the previous sections, we generate automated art price predictions (i.e.,ML) and then show that the relative magnitudes of the machine learning valuations help predict discrepancies between auction house presale estimates and transaction prices (i.e.,P−E$P-E$). However, is it possible todirectlypredict auction houses' under- and overvaluations? If auctioneers are affected by biases that are systematic and persistent, then past prediction errors should be informative about future prediction errors. Such autocorrelation of prediction errors can then exist at both the level of auction house experts and the level of artworks that the auctioneers are likely to consider as substitutes.
  To verify the plausibility of this hypothesis, we plot two histograms similar to that in Figure1with which we open this paper. We first sort lots (in the test set) based on the meanP−E$P-E$(in the training data) of both the artist and the “auctioneer,” where here we refer to a specific auction house-location combination (e.g., Christie's London). We then show the histograms forP−E$P-E$for the quartiles of lots associated with the lowest and highest average pastP−E$P-E$. Panel A of Figure12provides the results for the sort at the artist level. Clearly, lots by artists that have been valued relatively low (high) by auctioneers in recent years continue to receive relatively conservative (aggressive) presale estimates. While the averageP−E$P-E$equals −0.24 for artists associated with low past price-to-estimate ratios, it is virtually zero for artists with the highest recentP−E$P-E$.20Panel B compares “lowP−E$P-E$” to “highP−E$P-E$” auctioneers. Here, the persistence in prediction errors is also striking—auctioneers that issue relatively low estimates on average continue to do so.
  Encouraged by these findings, we let our neural network predict price-to-estimate ratios rather than prices. We use a network architecture that is identical to the one presented above, except we add a variable that captures the auction house presale estimate. We denote byMLP−E$ML_{P-E}$the resulting benchmark prediction—relying on both the image and all nonvisual characteristics—ofP−E$P-E$. In line with our earlier analysis, we can then study how auctioneers' prediction errors and buy-in rates correlate with our ex ante “prediction error prediction”MLP−E$ML_{P-E}$. The results are shown in columns (1), (3), and (5) of TableV. We find thatMLP−E$ML_{P-E}$correlates strongly with bothP−E$P-E$and the buy-in probability.
  The results are visualized in Figure13. The line illustrates the strong sensitivity of realized to predicted (log) price-to-estimate ratios. The triangles show that our ex ante predictions of price-versus-estimate discrepancies line up with buy-in probabilities. On both dimensions, the relation between predictions and auction outcomes is even stronger than in Figure10. Thus, using data on past errors, machine learning can help identify situations in which human experts are likely to be biased.
  To shed more light on what drives the predictability of auctioneers' prediction errors, we document theR2s of linear regressions ofP−E$P-E$on our benchmark prediction and different variations therein in TableVI. The first row shows that our predictionsMLP−E$ML_{P-E}$explain 6.7% of the variation inP−E$P-E$. The second row reports results for predictions generated without information on the presale estimate. TheR2only drops to 5.3%—a relatively minor change given the importance that one might have assumed the presale estimate to have in predicting the ex post realized price-to-estimate ratio. Thus, auctioneers' prediction errors are predictable from a truly ex ante perspective, that is, without even knowing the presale estimate of the auctioneer. The third row of TableVIalso drops information on the identity and location of the auction house. We see that theR2decreases to 3.4%. Thus, auction house effects are an important factor in the predictability of prediction errors. If we further drop information related to the artist or creation period, we see that the explanatory power deteriorates even more, as one might expect.
  Finally, in columns (2), (4), and (6) of TableV, we combine the prediction of the prediction error (i.e.,MLP−E$ML_{P-E}$) with the relative magnitude of the artwork valuation (i.e.,MLorth$ML_{orth}$) in one predictive model. We find thatMLP−E$ML_{P-E}$andMLorth$ML_{orth}$carry independent predictive power for auctioneers' prediction errors and buy-in rates.
article.VII. Discussion and Conclusion:
An abundant body of research links prices of infrequently traded “real” assets—artworks and other collectibles, but also real estate—to their quality-determining characteristics. In most such papers, predictability is not a goal in and of itself. Instead, most researchers are interested in the value of a hedonic characteristic, or use hedonic models to control for time-series variation in average quality when estimating price trends.21The practical usefulness of hedonic models in terms of asset valuation has arguably remained relatively limited, as market values of artworks or houses are not always well described by a linear function of their value-determining characteristics. Hedonic models simply cannot capture the complexity of art or real estate pricing. Hence, participants in markets for such assets have historically relied on the eyes and expertise of human valuers.
  The advent of machine learning is challenging this role of human expertise. It has already led to a range of new business models built on automated valuation methods, such as “iBuyers” (Buchak et al. (2022)). While our aim is not to come up with the best possible art price prediction algorithm—we could have collected additional information on artist fame and networks, artwork provenance, exhibition history, and so forth—our paper sheds light on the potential and determinants of price predictability in markets for illiquid real assets. One of the implications of our findings is that, even if modern machine learning techniques are unlikely to completely replace human judgment, they are likely to become important tools for investors and intermediaries as they have the ability to explain much of the variation in market values in a time-efficient and relatively inexpensive manner.
  Our work also shows how the asset valuations generated by machine learning can be used as a benchmark to evaluate human experts' valuations. We demonstrate that art auctioneers' presale estimates are informationally inefficient. The added value of machine learning is not uniform, but depends in a systematic way on a number of characteristics of the asset category, including the level of and dispersion in prices, and the availability of past transaction information.
  Moreover, tools similar to those used to predict prices can be employed to predict pricing errors. We find that art auction houses are systematically biased in predictable ways. Whether the “predictable prediction errors” of auctioneers stem mainly from behavioral versus strategic biases is not easy to determine. As we explain in the opening paragraphs of this paper, the effects of these two types of biases may be similar. More fundamentally, however, we lack a good theoretical understanding of (i) what determines optimal estimates in a setting in which auction houses compete for consignments and both consignors' and bidders' behavior may be affected by the presale estimate, and (ii) how deviating from “honesty” could be an equilibrium longer run strategy for auctioneers. This definitely represents an avenue for further research.
  Overall, however, we believe that the predictability shown in SectionVIof this paper is likely to be related to a combination of different factors. The fact that auction house effects are so important in explaining prediction errors and buy-in rates suggests that different auction houses implement different strategies in terms of presale estimates. There may thus exist heterogeneity in how auctioneers weigh the costs and benefits of higher versus lower estimates. Other results in this paper point more to the importance of behavioral frictions, in particular artist-level persistence—across all auction houses—in prediction errors.
  Whatever are the precise sources of nonfundamental variation in auction house estimates, these inefficiencies and biases are not just a side show. In settings in which buyers and sellers are affected by human experts' appraisals, the discrepancy between these appraisals and an unbiased proxy for market values correlates with relevant economic outcomes. For example, when consignors set reserve prices in line with auction houses' expectations, buy-in rates will be higher if auctioneers are too optimistic, which is indeed what we find. But bidders may also anchor on auction house estimates. While it is difficult to formally quantify the extent to which this occurs, one resulting prediction in the context of our analysis is that higher relative automated valuations—or, equivalently, less aggressive presale estimates—are associated with higher postacquisition returns. We test this hypothesis using a small sample of artworks that we could identify that initially traded in 2015 (and thus are part of our test data set) and thatretradedover the time period 2016 to 2018. Column (1) of TableVIIregresses annualized log returns against the initial log price-to-estimate ratios (i.e.,P−E$P-E$). We see a strong negative correlation between the relative level of the purchase price and the postacquisition return. Column (2) then adds our orthogonalized automated valuation (i.e.,MLorth$ML_{orth}$). In line with our hypothesis, higher automated valuations relative to presale estimates are associated with (weakly) higher returns.
  In sum, it is clear that automated valuation methods (and predictions of human errors) may be useful for both buyers and sellers in markets for illiquid real assets. It will be interesting to see how they change equilibrium behavior and outcomes in such markets in the future.
  Editors: Stefan Nagel, Philip Bond, Amit Seru, and Wei Xiong
